tools.name: IBM AI Fairness 360 (AIF360)
tools.description: Comprehensive open-source Python/R library for detecting, understanding, and mitigating bias in ML models throughout the AI lifecycle. Includes 70+ fairness metrics and 11+ bias mitigation algorithms.
tools.relevance: Core tool for any AI assurance practice — enables quantitative fairness assessment across demographic groups. Essential for credit scoring, insurance pricing, and lending model validation.

tools.name: Microsoft Fairlearn
tools.description: Python package to assess and improve fairness of ML models. Part of Microsoft's broader Responsible AI Toolbox. Focuses on allocation harms and quality-of-service harms.
tools.relevance: Complementary to AIF360 — different philosophical approach to fairness (focuses on constrained optimization). Good for Azure-based deployments.

tools.name: Google TensorFlow Fairness Indicators
tools.description: Open-source library and dashboard for evaluating ML model fairness at scale. Part of Google's Responsible AI Toolkit. Designed for production-scale evaluation.
tools.relevance: Best suited for TensorFlow-based models. The dashboard visualization is useful for communicating fairness results to non-technical stakeholders.

tools.name: Giskard
tools.description: Open-source evaluation and testing library for LLM agents. Provides transparency, accountability, and helps ensure quality, security, and compliance. Recognized in 2024 GitHub Accelerator.
tools.relevance: Particularly relevant for testing LLM-based applications in financial services (chatbots, advisory tools). Includes hallucination detection and robustness testing.

tools.name: Guardrails AI
tools.description: Python framework for building reliable AI applications. Performs input/output validation, risk detection and mitigation in prompts and responses. Includes validator library for specific risk types.
tools.relevance: Implementation tool for LLM deployment guardrails — directly applicable to financial services deploying customer-facing AI.

tools.name: MAS Veritas Toolkit v2.0
tools.description: Open-source tool developed by MAS consortium to help financial institutions assess AI solutions against FEAT principles (Fairness, Ethics, Accountability, Transparency). Includes methodologies for each principle.
tools.relevance: The most directly applicable tool for financial services AI governance. Should be adapted for Australian regulatory context.

tools.name: FINOS AI Governance Framework
tools.description: Open-source initiative for developing governance frameworks for onboarding, developing, and running AI solutions within financial services. Aims to align with regional regulations including EU AI Act.
tools.relevance: Industry-specific governance framework for financial services. Worth monitoring and potentially contributing to.

tools.name: MLflow
tools.description: Open-source platform for ML lifecycle management. Includes experiment tracking, model registry, deployment management, and evaluation capabilities. Integrates governance and compliance features.
tools.relevance: Foundation for ML governance infrastructure — model versioning, lineage tracking, and reproducibility are prerequisites for AI assurance.

tools.name: Holistic AI Platform
tools.description: Commercial platform providing AI risk management, compliance, and governance capabilities. Includes bias auditing, explainability, and regulatory mapping tools.
tools.relevance: One of the leading commercial AI governance platforms. Useful reference for what capabilities an AI assurance practice should offer.

tools.name: Aequitas
tools.description: Open-source bias audit toolkit developed by the Center for Data Science and Public Policy. Provides group fairness metrics and bias reports for ML models.
tools.relevance: Lightweight alternative to AIF360 for quick fairness audits. Good for initial assessments before deeper analysis.

tools.name: ValidMind
tools.description: AI governance platform specifically designed for regulated financial services. Configurable governance templates, comprehensive model inventory tracking for traditional and GenAI, 300+ pre-built tests and validation metrics. Partnered with Experian for automated AI compliance. Supports SR 11-7, EU AI Act, SS1/23 compliance.
tools.relevance: The most relevant commercial platform for financial services AI governance. Its Experian partnership and pre-built regulatory compliance templates make it particularly interesting for banks and insurers.

tools.name: Credo AI
tools.description: Enterprise AI governance, risk, and compliance platform. Named Forrester Wave Leader in AI Governance Solutions Q3 2025. Pre-built policy packs for EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001. Features risk scoring, compliance dashboards, and audit reporting across agents, models, and applications.
tools.relevance: Market-leading AI governance platform with strong analyst recognition. Its policy pack approach to regulatory compliance is efficient but may lack depth for complex financial services requirements.

tools.name: Lumenova AI
tools.description: AI governance software with pre-configured frameworks (NIST AI RMF, other regulatory standards). AI directory, automated compliance checks, fairness/reliability/security evaluation. Focus on reducing manual audit effort.
tools.relevance: Good mid-market option for financial institutions wanting automated AI compliance without the complexity of enterprise platforms.

tools.name: Dynamo AI
tools.description: End-to-end compliant generative AI infrastructure. Backed by consortium of top financial institutions. Focus on safe, secure, and compliant GenAI deployment. Guardrail Playbook for creating, evaluating, and monitoring AI guardrails in regulated enterprises.
tools.relevance: Unique positioning: backed by financial institutions for financial institutions. Their guardrail approach is practical and directly deployable. Worth evaluating as a recommended vendor for consulting engagements.

tools.name: IBM watsonx.governance
tools.description: IBM's enterprise AI governance platform. Tracks AI model lifecycle, detects bias, manages risk, ensures regulatory compliance. Partnered with Deloitte for operationalising AI governance in enterprise settings.
tools.relevance: Strong enterprise play, especially for institutions already in IBM ecosystem. Deloitte partnership creates a go-to-market that smaller practices need to differentiate against.

tools.name: Enkrypt AI
tools.description: Specialised AI red teaming platform with specific focus on autonomous financial AI systems. Agent-based red teaming for exposing vulnerabilities in multi-agent financial systems. Automated adversarial testing capabilities.
tools.relevance: One of the few platforms specifically targeting agentic AI red teaming in financial services. As institutions deploy AI agents, this capability gap will become critical.

tools.name: FINOS AI Governance Framework v2 (with Agentic AI Extension)
tools.description: Open-source, machine-readable AI governance framework specifically for financial services. Version 2 covers 25 risks and mitigations including agentic AI. Backed by BMO, Citibank, Morgan Stanley, RBC, Microsoft, Google Cloud, AWS. Designed for programmatic compliance checking and CI/CD integration.
tools.relevance: The most credible open-source FS-specific AI governance framework. Machine-readable format enables automation. Contributing positions a practice as an industry participant.

tools.name: US Treasury FS AI RMF Control Framework
tools.description: 230 control objectives across four NIST AI RMF functions (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage) tailored for financial services. Includes AI Adoption Stage Questionnaire, Risk and Control Matrix, Implementation Guidebook, and Control Objective Reference Guide.
tools.relevance: The most comprehensive control framework for FS AI governance. 230 objectives provide natural gap assessment baseline. Will become de facto US bank examination standard.

tools.name: NeuralTrust AI Red Teaming Platform
tools.description: Platform for automated adversarial testing of GenAI systems in banking and financial services. Supports prompt injection testing, data exfiltration detection, bias probing, and continuous security monitoring integrated into MLOps pipelines.
tools.relevance: Specialised AI security testing for banking. Integrates into existing DevSecOps workflows which is important for enterprise adoption.

tools.name: CALM: Comprehensive Assessment of LLM-as-a-Judge Biases
tools.description: Automated framework for systematically quantifying biases in LLM-as-a-judge applications. Tests for position bias, verbosity bias, self-enhancement bias, and other systematic evaluation errors. Essential for validating LLM-based evaluation pipelines.
tools.relevance: Critical for any assurance practice using LLM-based evaluation. Without bias quantification, LLM-as-judge results are unreliable. Builds toward production-grade evaluation validation.

tools.name: SHADE-Arena
tools.description: Comprehensive sabotage evaluation framework for agentic AI. 17 complex tasks with paired malicious side-tasks, monitor AI evaluation, chain-of-thought analysis. Evaluates both sabotage capability and monitoring effectiveness.
tools.relevance: critical
tools.financialServicesApplicability: Direct — financial services sabotage scenarios (banking operations, information security) included. Framework adaptable for sector-specific evaluation.

tools.name: METR 50%-Task-Completion Time Horizon
tools.description: Metric measuring AI capability in human-equivalent task completion time. Provides intuitive capability trajectory (7-month doubling) useful for governance planning. NeurIPS 2025 accepted methodology.
tools.relevance: high
tools.financialServicesApplicability: Useful for capability trajectory planning. Extrapolation helps calibrate governance frameworks for future AI capability levels. Currently focused on software tasks — adaptation needed for financial services workflows.

tools.name: 4D AI Fluency Framework
tools.developer: Rick Dakan, Joe Feller (in collaboration with Anthropic)
tools.description: 24-behaviour taxonomy for measuring safe and effective human-AI collaboration. 11 behaviours directly observable in AI conversations. Provides baseline for measuring AI fluency development over time.
tools.relevance: moderate
tools.financialServicesApplicability: Adaptable for financial services AI readiness assessments. Could form basis for staff AI competency requirements and training program design.

tools.name: OECD AI Incidents and Hazards Monitor
tools.description: OECD's publicly accessible database tracking and categorising AI-related incidents and hazards globally. Provides taxonomised incident data useful for risk assessment, benchmarking, and learning from real-world AI failures.
tools.relevance: Valuable reference for AI incident readiness assessments. Can benchmark institutional AI incident taxonomy against the OECD's classification. Useful for identifying patterns and emerging risk categories.

tools.name: Dynamic Safety Case Management System (DSCMS)
tools.description: Proposed framework combining checkable safety arguments with Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) for continuous AI safety assurance. Uses LLM-based Delphi method for probability estimation of safety sub-claims. Automates safety case monitoring and triggers reviews when thresholds are breached.
tools.relevance: Represents the frontier of continuous AI safety assurance methodology. The SPI approach is directly applicable to financial services AI monitoring. The LLM-based Delphi method for confidence estimation could transform how safety cases are evaluated.

tools.name: Microsoft Foundry Control Plane + Agent 365
tools.description: Enterprise AI agent governance platform consolidating inventory, observability, compliance with AI guardrail policies, and security into a single interface. Foundry agent tracing provides runtime trace diagnostics. Agent 365 Observability SDK (OTel-based) emits telemetry into governance workflows. Manages agent sprawl, identity controls, and policy enforcement across tenant.
tools.relevance: Represents the frontier of enterprise AI observability. Financial institutions in the Microsoft ecosystem should evaluate for agentic AI governance. Sets the standard for what AI observability infrastructure should look like.

tools.name: Scotiabank Ethics Assistant
tools.description: AI ethics review tool developed by Scotiabank in collaboration with Deloitte Canada (launched 2022). Proactively embeds ethical considerations into AI/ML projects from design stage. Supports mandatory ethics review process with tiered risk-based approval. Part of comprehensive Data Ethics framework integrated into corporate Code of Conduct.
tools.relevance: One of the few documented ethics review tools in production at a major bank. Model for how financial institutions can operationalise ethics review at scale. Winner of DataIQ Best Responsible AI Program 2024.

tools.name: ETSI TS 104 008 — Continuous Auditing Based Conformity Assessment (CABCA)
tools.description: ETSI's January 2026 technical specification for continuous auditing-based conformity assessment (CABCA) of AI-enabled systems. Defines recurring assessment cycles, automated evidence collection, machine-readable conformity metrics, and dynamic conformity status updates. Supports self-assessment, third-party assessment, and certification use cases. Directly aligned with EU AI Act post-market monitoring obligations. The framework operationalises 'living compliance' for dynamic AI systems that evolve through retraining and data pipeline changes.
tools.relevance: The technical infrastructure for continuous AI compliance — operationalises post-market monitoring obligations under EU AI Act and replaces point-in-time audit models

tools.name: MAS MindForge AI Risk Management Toolkit (Phase 2)
tools.description: Comprehensive AI risk management toolkit developed by a 24-firm consortium under MAS's Project MindForge, published March 20, 2026. Includes: AI Risk Management Operationalisation Handbook (four sections: scope/oversight, AI risk management, AI lifecycle management, enablers); AI Risk Management Implementation Examples (case studies from financial institutions); AI Risk Management Executive Handbook. Covers traditional AI, generative AI, and agentic AI. Periodically updated through the BuildFin.ai workgroup.
tools.relevance: Most operationally detailed AI risk management toolkit for financial services globally — applicable directly to Australian bank, insurer, and asset manager AI governance programs

tools.name: DNV-RP-0671: Recommended Practice for Assurance of AI-Enabled Systems
tools.description: DNV's recommended practice for assuring AI-enabled systems, part of DNV's Digital Trust documentation suite. Lifecycle-based framework covering design, development, testing, deployment, and ongoing monitoring of AI systems. Distinguishes institutional trust from technical reliability. Treats data quality as foundational to AI assurance. Provides structured vocabulary for AI-specific failure modes including distributional shift, model degradation, and adversarial robustness. Supports independent third-party AI assurance.
tools.relevance: Credible independent AI assurance framework from a technically respected institution — provides methodology for sector-specific AI assurance engagements in banking and finance

tools.name: OECD Six-Step Due Diligence Framework for Responsible AI
tools.description: OECD's voluntary six-step responsible business conduct due diligence framework applied to AI, published February 19, 2026. Steps: (1) embed RBC into policies/management systems; (2) identify/assess actual and potential adverse impacts; (3) cease/prevent/mitigate impacts; (4) track implementation and results; (5) communicate actions taken; (6) provide/cooperate in remediation. Risk-agnostic and applicable across all AI systems and sectors. Provides interoperability mapping to EU AI Act, ASEAN AI Governance Guide, and Korean AI Basic Act. For multinational enterprises involved in the AI value chain.
tools.relevance: International baseline for AI due diligence expectations across multinational enterprises — soft law that may harden; relevant for Australian firms with international operations or clients

tools.name: NIST AI 800-4: Six-Category Post-Deployment Monitoring Framework
tools.source: NIST Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI), March 2026
tools.description: Six-category taxonomy for post-deployment AI monitoring: Functionality (does it work as intended?), Operational (consistent service?), Human Factors (transparent and high quality?), Security (protected from attacks?), Compliance (adheres to regulations?), Large-Scale Impacts (promotes human flourishing?). Includes gaps, barriers, and open questions for each category.
tools.maturity: Authoritative — first federal systematic mapping from US national standards body
tools.applicability: AI assurance methodology; post-deployment audit design; monitoring gap assessment

tools.name: Chiodo et al. HITL Computational Classification (Total/Many-One/Turing Reductions)
tools.description: Formal classification of Human-in-the-Loop setups using computability theory: (1) Trivial monitoring (total functions) — human can only stop the system, no genuine computational influence; (2) Many-one reductions — single endpoint human decision; (3) Turing reductions — rich, iterative human-AI interaction. Used to assess legal adequacy and safety value of HITL designs.
tools.applicability: HITL audit design; regulatory adequacy assessment; liability exposure analysis

tools.name: Jaton Ground Truth Tracing Methodology
tools.description: Methodology for examining algorithms in light of their ground truth datasets. Involves tracing the sociotechnical process by which training labels were assigned — who annotated, what instructions were given, what disagreements were resolved and how, what sociocultural fingerprints are embedded. Provides a 'ground truth studies' research program for assessing the epistemic foundation of AI accuracy claims.
tools.maturity: Academic methodology — requires adaptation for commercial use
tools.applicability: Trust provenance assessment; training data audits; epistemic validity of accuracy metrics

tools.name: Baum-Laux Causal Oversight Taxonomy (HITL/HOTL/HO with Temporal Modes)
tools.description: A causal taxonomy replacing spatial 'loop' metaphors with precise structural definitions. HITL: human is constitutively necessary (gate). HOTL: human is externally corrective (switch) with three temporal modes: synchronous (real-time), asynchronous (periodic), anticipatory (pre-deployment constraint-setting). Human Oversight: normative HOTL requiring preparedness and capacity, not just positioning. Second dimension: complementary vs. hybrid intelligence. Four structural configurations (HITL+Complementary, HITL+Hybrid, HOTL+Complementary, HOTL+Hybrid). Role duality mitigation: layered oversight architecture across real-time, systemic, and compliance layers.
tools.applicability: Classifying AI governance arrangements, regulatory compliance assessment for EU AI Act Article 14, designing oversight architectures for agentic AI systems at scale

tools.name: Operational Agency Graph (OAG) — Culpability Tracing in AI Systems
tools.description: A graphical evidentiary methodology for tracing liability through AI systems. Typed nodes: Natural Person, Juridical Person, AI Agent (non-person profiled via Operational Agency pillars). Edge attributes: Direction (causal flow), Type (qualitative interaction description), Weight (legal significance derived from three OA pillars). Three analytical pillars for AI Agent nodes: (1) Goal-Directedness — proxy for intent (objective functions, reward functions, design choices); (2) Predictive Processing — proxy for foreseeability (AI's own risk logs, red-teaming outputs, embedded training knowledge); (3) Safety Architecture — proxy for standard of care (risk-utility analysis of design choices, availability of reasonable alternatives). Applied to tort, civil rights, constitutional law, and antitrust cases.
tools.applicability: Post-incident liability analysis, AI governance documentation strategy (safety architecture as legal defence), regulatory investigations of AI-caused harms in financial services

tools.name: Warrant Attestation Levels (WAL-0 to WAL-3) — Trust Provenance Certification Framework
tools.description: A graduated adoption framework for cryptographic data provenance in AI agent systems, analogous to SLSA build levels for software supply chains. WAL-0: No provenance guarantees (current default for most agent systems). WAL-1: Source identification recorded, post-hoc attribution possible. WAL-2: Sources sign responses with ECDSA/Ed25519; source keys certified by Domain WCA; provenance layer verifies signatures. WAL-3: Full reference monitor properties (complete mediation, tamperproofness, verifiability); all tool calls logged in hash-chained attestation log; non-interference enforced; TLA+-verified self-licensing prevention. Use case table: Exploratory research WAL-0, General info retrieval WAL-1, Business intelligence WAL-2, Medical/Legal/Financial trading WAL-3.
tools.applicability: Trust provenance certification for high-stakes AI systems; financial services compliance (credit decisions, trading, prudential AI); regulatory requirements for auditable AI data sourcing

tools.name: Runtime Policy Engine Architecture — Path-Level Agent Governance
tools.description: A formal architecture for runtime AI agent governance based on policy functions πj(agent_identity, partial_path, proposed_action, shared_governance_state) → [0,1]. Key components: (1) Registration Phase: evaluate all path-independent policies (agent integrity, documentation, scheduling) before any task starts; (2) Per-Step Phase: intercept proposed actions, evaluate path-dependent policies, compute fleet violation score, apply decision function (Pass/Steer/Block); (3) Shared Governance State Σ: cross-agent ledger for information barrier enforcement; (4) Audit Trail: deterministic log enabling independent reproduction of all enforcement decisions. Concrete policy templates: PII predecessor requirement, approval before external actions, data exfiltration prevention (graduated), information barrier enforcement (requires Σ), execution bounds. Governance objective: max expected utility subject to E[violation score] ≤ B (risk budget).
tools.applicability: Enterprise agentic AI governance; financial services information barrier compliance; EU AI Act Article 9/12/14 implementation; designing runtime governance infrastructure for multi-agent systems

tools.name: NatWest Two-Stage AI Governance Model — Enterprise Implementation Pattern
tools.description: A pragmatic enterprise AI governance model validated at scale in a major bank. Stage 1: Structured risk assessment template applied to all AI use cases before development (purpose, data sources, potential risks, bias indicators) — provides early visibility and consistency. Stage 2: Weekly cross-functional expert working group (privacy, risk, security, legal) reviews Stage 1 assessments; approved use cases proceed to detailed secondary assessment before production. Key success factors: governance embedded at every stage (not final gate only); cross-functional expertise in review (not just compliance); framework designed as enabler not barrier; results in repeatable, reliable model embedded in business-as-usual processes. Limitations: human-intensive, suited to bounded use cases, not yet adapted for runtime agentic governance.
tools.applicability: Enterprise AI governance programme design; pre-deployment AI use case assessment; building governance culture and repeatable frameworks; model for APRA CPS 230/234 AI governance requirements

tools.name: Ground Truth Register
tools.description: A governance artifact that records the origin of each target variable, label definition, proxy choice, labeling process, disagreement rule, and condition under which the target should be reconsidered. Designed to distinguish true ground truth from organizational assumptions and operational proxies.
tools.source: Derived from p118, p125, and p129
tools.applicability: High-value for regulated AI systems using socially constructed labels such as fraud, risk, vulnerability, intent, suitability, or quality. Particularly useful in financial services, hiring, healthcare, and public sector triage.

tools.name: Trust Property Decay Schedule
tools.description: A control framework that classifies trust claims by how they degrade: certify once, periodically revalidate, or continuously monitor. Each claim is paired with evidentiary owners, monitoring signals, and trigger conditions for re-assessment.
tools.source: Derived from p119, p124, and p129
tools.applicability: Useful for moving from one-off model approval to lifecycle assurance in agentic systems, especially where institutions need to separate static attestations from runtime behavioral claims.

tools.name: Proof Drill Evidence Packet
tools.description: A bounded case file for one AI-influenced decision or action, containing the triggering input, system output, model/build identifier, active policy or prompt state, human interventions, downstream action, and any subsequent remediation. Used to test whether oversight is reconstructable under time pressure.
tools.source: Derived from p127, supported by p118 and p128
tools.applicability: Suitable for internal audit, regulatory examination prep, model risk management, incident response, and board assurance in organizations deploying consequential AI workflows.

tools.name: AEF-1: Minimum Operating Conditions for Independent Third Party AI Evaluations
tools.description: A voluntary standard and compliance checklist published by the AI Evaluation Foundation defining minimum operating conditions for third-party AI evaluators. Covers five core principles: sufficient access and resources, minimized conflicts of interest, analytic autonomy, transparent methods and results, and protection of sensitive information. Provides a machine-readable checklist that evaluators can use to demonstrate baseline independence, access, and transparency.
tools.utility: Immediately usable as a procurement specification: any third-party AI evaluation cited as assurance should be assessed against the AEF-1 checklist. Provides concrete criteria that don't require deep technical expertise to verify.

tools.name: AI Bill of Materials (AI BOM) via SPDX 3.0
tools.description: An extension of the Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) concept to AI systems. Using the SPDX 3.0 AI and Dataset Profiles, an AI BOM documents: algorithms, training data sources, preprocessing steps, model architecture, hyperparameters, fine-tuning procedures, deployment configuration, licensing, and sub-component dependencies. The AI Profile captures model-specific metadata (autonomy type, domain, safety risk assessment, build-time provenance); the Dataset Profile captures data provenance (collection process, known biases, confidentiality level, update mechanisms). Mandated by EU AI Act transparency requirements and emerging as a de facto standard for AI supply chain transparency.
tools.utility: Should become a mandatory procurement requirement for all AI systems in financial services. Enables institutions to see inside the "black box" of AI vendor offerings and identify supply chain risks at procurement time rather than post-deployment.

tools.name: AgentFacts / Know Your Agent (KYA)
tools.description: A universal metadata standard for AI agent transparency and verification, analogous to KYC in financial services. AgentFacts provides cryptographically-signed capability declarations from multiple authorities, dynamic permission management with TTL freshness guarantees, and a ten-section schema covering identity, model transparency, classification, capabilities, authentication, compliance, performance, supply chain (SBOM integration), and verification. The multi-authority model distributes trust verification across specialized authorities rather than relying on self-declaration.
tools.utility: Addresses the fourth/fifth-party AI risk (Shadow AI embedded in SaaS tools) that existing TPRM frameworks cannot see. Financial institutions should adopt KYA requirements in AI procurement standards as the AI agent ecosystem matures.

tools.name: Three-Question Audit Diagnostic (Homewood et al.)
tools.description: A diagnostic framework for evaluating whether any AI review — internal audit, external evaluation, vendor self-assessment, or regulatory examination — provides genuine assurance or merely the appearance of it. Three questions: (1) Who is reviewing? — Scope of independence (financial, structural, expertise-based). (2) What are they reviewing? — Depth of assessment (documentation, independent testing, or production observation). (3) What can they actually see? — Access constraints (weights, training data, scaffolding, intermediate states). The framework reveals that most commercial AI reviews score high on scope but low on depth and independence.
tools.utility: Immediately usable in any AI assurance engagement. Transforms abstract claims about review quality into specific, answerable questions. Financial institutions should require written answers to all three questions before accepting any external AI evaluation as sufficient assurance.

tools.name: Accountability Horizon Mapping
tools.description: A methodology derived from Cobbe, Veale & Singh's accountability horizon concept. For each AI system, map: what the institution can see and is accountable for (within horizon), what the institution cannot see but is affected by (beyond horizon), and what the institution is accountable for but cannot see (obligation gap). The mapping should cover: direct vendors (first tier), vendor's AI providers (second tier), vendor's software dependencies (third tier), open source components (fourth tier), and infrastructure providers (fifth tier). For each tier, document the visibility and liability allocation.
tools.utility: A board-ready methodology for making accountability horizon risks concrete. Creates a structured document that can inform AI governance committee decisions, regulatory engagement, and risk appetite definition. Should be updated annually or on material AI system changes.

tools.name: Synthetic Reality Stack
tools.description: Ferrara's layered model for analysing how generative AI erodes trust across content, identity, interaction, and institutions. Useful for locating where verification breaks down and which control layer is actually being targeted.
tools.applicability: Threat modelling and control design for environments where provenance and identity verification are central, including customer communication, document workflows, and public-facing AI channels.

tools.name: Role-Sensitive Oversight Lens
tools.description: A design lens derived from Shi & DiFranzo that distinguishes oversight needs by agent role and harm type, especially action-risk versus meaning-risk. Helps avoid one-size-fits-all human control requirements.
tools.applicability: Scoping oversight requirements for agentic systems with materially different social and operational functions.

tools.name: Solve–Verify Asymmetry Map
tools.description: A practical method for classifying tasks by whether they are hard to solve and easy to verify, or easy to generate and hard to verify. Used to decide where human review is viable, where abstention is needed, and where workflows should be redesigned.
tools.applicability: Human oversight architecture, escalation design, and approval workflow redesign for high-stakes AI use cases.

tools.name: Expectation–Realisation Gap Review
tools.description: A planning and assurance method that compares forecast productivity gains from agentic AI to realised outcomes after accounting for verification burden, workflow friction, and heterogeneous benefit distribution.
tools.applicability: Business case review, executive challenge, and post-deployment benefit validation for AI agents and copilots.

tools.name: HITL/HOTL/HOT Oversight Mode Framework
tools.description: Three-tier framework for human-AI oversight at scale: (1) HITL = human must approve before action (high-risk decisions), (2) HOTL = human monitors, can intervene after (medium-risk), (3) HOT = full automation (low-risk, high-volume). Agentic workflows require dynamic switching between modes based on risk context.

tools.name: Epistemic Compression and Regime Index
tools.description: Framework from Lukas et al. for diagnosing when AI model complexity is a liability vs. asset. The Regime Index scores domains on temporal stability, context invariance, data-to-complexity ratio, ground truth quality, and causal prior strength. Scores 3-5 = Shifting Regime (Compression Mandatory — simple models outperform complex); 0-1 = Stable Regime (Complexity Viable — deep learning earns its keep). The key insight: most high-stakes financial services applications are in the Shifting Regime.

tools.name: Solve-Verify Asymmetry Analysis
tools.description: Framework from CSIRO (Zhu) for determining where human oversight is feasible and meaningful. Some tasks are hard to solve but easy to verify (where HITL works); others are easy to generate but hard to verify (where HITL fails or requires special safeguards). Meaningful oversight = designing systems so human review is concentrated where verification is feasible, consequential, and decision-changing.

tools.name: MAS Thematic Review Methodology
tools.description: Regulatory empiricism approach: conduct structured thematic reviews of actual AI governance practices across supervised institutions, document observed good practice, and publish as industry guidance. MAS conducted this in mid-2024 across banks' AI model risk management practices and published findings in December 2024.

tools.name: Challenge-and-Response Approval Protocol
tools.description: HITL operational practice requiring positive acknowledgment of multiple checklist items (intent, data lineage, permissions chain, expected blast radius, rollback plan) before approving high-risk AI actions. Replaces passive 'Approve?' prompts with active verification.

tools.name: Time-Boxed Decision Lanes
tools.description: Risk-tiered SLAs for human approvers: 15-second lane for low-risk actions, 2-minute lane for PII access, 15-minute lane for financial disbursements. If approval times out, fail-safe to denied and capture partial context for audit. Prevents bottleneck-induced bypass of human oversight.

tools.name: MindForge AI Risk Management Toolkit
tools.description: Multi-phase consortium-developed AI risk management toolkit produced by 24 financial institutions under MAS coordination. Includes an Operationalisation Handbook organised around four sections (Scope and Oversight, AI Risk Management, AI Lifecycle Management, Enablers) and AI case studies. The most concrete, implementable financial services AI governance framework produced to date.

tools.name: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)
tools.description: Explainability technique for AI model decisions. Provides consistent feature importance scores that sum to the difference between expected model output and actual prediction. Standard operational requirement under 2026 AI governance trends for regulated use cases. Must be integrated into production pipelines, not used post-hoc.

tools.name: LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations)
tools.description: Technique for explaining individual predictions of any classifier or regressor by learning local models around each prediction. Complementary to SHAP. Standard operational requirement for explainability in regulated AI use cases.

tools.name: Counterfactual Explanations
tools.description: Explainability technique that identifies minimal changes to input features that would change the AI decision outcome. Particularly relevant for fairness and non-discrimination in credit, insurance, HR AI decisions. EU AI Act Article 13 requires systems to be able to explain their outputs; counterfactuals are a standard method for satisfying this in high-stakes decisions.

tools.name: Model Drift Detection
tools.description: Continuous monitoring methodology for detecting when AI model statistical properties diverge from training conditions. Three types: data drift (input distribution changes), concept drift (relationship between inputs and outputs changes), and upstream data drift (data collection changes). Must be continuous, not periodic, per 2026 governance trends. Performance degradation is particularly dangerous because systems often operate "well enough" until significant harm has occurred.

tools.name: Model Cards
tools.description: Standardised documentation format for AI model metadata: architecture, intended use, performance metrics, risks, limitations, training data characteristics. Required during audits under EU AI Act and emerging US state frameworks (Colorado, California). Model cards become core compliance artefacts.

tools.name: Data Lineage Tracking
tools.description: Tracks full lifecycle of model data: sources, transformations, access controls, and usage. Maps relationships between model and all components contributing to its development. Moves firmly into audit scope in 2026. Required for high-risk AI systems under EU AI Act.

tools.name: Meaningful Human Control (MHC) Framework
tools.description: Three-pillar framework for operationalising human oversight of AI: (1) technical architectures with embedded human control mechanisms; (2) human-AI interactions optimised for human understanding; (3) evolution of AI systems harmonising intelligence with controllability. Governance must prioritise human-centred participatory frameworks transcending technical elite dominance.

tools.name: Knowledge Sanctuaries
tools.description: Proposed by Marchal et al. (Google DeepMind) as spaces where human epistemic independence is protected from AI-mediated knowledge environments. Part of the socio-epistemic infrastructure needed to prevent cognitive deskilling and "epistemic drift" when AI agents become primary knowledge intermediaries. Governance concept for protecting human epistemic resilience.

tools.name: Baier Goodwill Theory of Trust
tools.description: Annette Baier's philosophical theory distinguishing trust from mere reliance. Trust requires the trustee to have goodwill toward the trustor — the motivation to act in the trustor's interests. Trust creates vulnerability obligations that reliability does not. Used by Carroll (2025) to argue that AI should be reliable not trustworthy. Central to the provenance of trust question.

tools.name: AI Lifecycle Attestation Chain
tools.description: Cryptographic attestation pattern binding training data, model weights, quantization, deployment state, and inference outputs into one verifiable chain. Designed to detect silent substitution, tampering, and output provenance disputes.

tools.name: Verifiable AI Provenance (VAP) Framework
tools.description: Framework for cryptographically verifiable AI decision provenance using hash chains, digital signatures, external anchors, evidence packs, and privacy-preserving verification. Built for high-risk AI systems needing third-party auditability.

tools.name: Completeness Invariant
tools.description: Audit design pattern requiring every attempt event to have exactly one corresponding outcome event, making selective logging and narrative reconstruction easier to detect. Useful for regulatory evidence packs and incident reconstruction.

tools.name: Human Override Coverage
tools.description: Metric for quantifying the share of AI outputs reviewed, modified, approved, or rejected by a human professional. Useful for converting vague oversight claims into auditable evidence, though it must be paired with capacity and quality measures.

tools.name: PURE Framework
tools.description: DBS ethical decision framework for AI and data use: Purposeful, Unsurprising, Respectful, Explainable. Used as a practical filter inside broader data foundations and model governance processes.

tools.name: Digital Value Capture for AI
tools.description: Measurement discipline linking AI use cases to explicit business outcomes, capital allocation, and course correction. Relevant because sustainable governance at scale requires economic legibility alongside control effectiveness.

tools.name: Learned Hand Formula for AI Negligence
tools.description: The Learned Hand formula (B < P × L) defines negligence as when the burden of precautions (B) is less than the probability of harm (P) multiplied by the gravity of loss (L). Applied to AI: B represents the effort required by operators, deployers, or developers to prevent foreseeable risks; P is the probability the AI system will cause harm; L is the magnitude of potential harm. The formula breaks down for AI in two ways: B approaches infinity when AI operates beyond human cognitive capacity (making the formula mathematically void or indicating no negligence); and B is undefined for developers of neural networks, where logic is induced rather than written. For AI liability analysis, the formula's primary value is in shifting the negligence question from 'did a human intervene?' to 'was the precaution burden reasonable given the harm probability and magnitude?'

tools.name: Three-Pillar AI Oversight Duty Framework
tools.description: A legal framework for operationalizing AI oversight duties, proposed by Htin (p165), that allocates distinct duties across the AI supply chain. Pillar 1: Deployer's duty to implement genuine Human-Systems Integration (HSI) frameworks — not passive monitoring roles but active human-AI collaboration architectures designed for human cognitive capacity. Pillar 2: Developer's duty to demonstrate technical robustness — including interpretability tooling, testing for known failure modes, and documentation of model behaviour boundaries. Pillar 3: Both parties' duty for post-market monitoring and failure reporting — continuous surveillance of model behaviour in deployment environment, with mandatory reporting of adverse events. This three-way allocation resolves the liability sponge problem by making each party's specific duty legally enforceable, rather than concentrating accountability on the human operator who lacks realistic intervention capacity.

tools.name: Human-Systems Integration (HSI) Frameworks for AI
tools.description: Human-Systems Integration frameworks ensure AI systems are designed for genuine human oversight rather than performative human presence. Multiple HSI frameworks exist; the most directly applicable for AI oversight is the Partnership on AI's Human-AI Collaboration Framework — a 36-question heuristic for designing and assessing human-AI collaborative systems. Key HSI design principles: friction roles (forcing active human deliberation before AI decisions to prevent automation bias), resilience roles (positioning humans as fail-safe sources of contextual judgment at system boundaries), and cognitive handrails (providing uncertainty quantification, saliency indicators, and model confidence signals calibrated for human cognition). HSI frameworks operationalise the first-pillar duty: a deployer who places a human in a loop without HSI-designed interfaces is negligent regardless of whether a human was technically present.

tools.name: Crootof-Kaminski-Price Human Role Taxonomy
tools.description: A taxonomy of human roles in AI oversight developed by Crootof, Kaminski, and Price (referenced in p165) to help courts adjudicate whether a human was truly 'in the loop' or was merely a liability sponge. The taxonomy categorises human roles in automated systems, distinguishing meaningful oversight roles from friction roles (designed to force deliberate slowing of AI decisions) and resilience roles (positioning humans as fail-safe sources of contextual judgment). The taxonomy's legal significance is that it provides courts with a framework to distinguish genuine oversight from nominal oversight — helping resolve the accountability gap by identifying whether the human's role was structurally capable of catching the failure that occurred.

tools.name: Taddeo Trust Quantification
tools.description: Taddeo's framework (2007, 2010) treats trust as a quantitative property of reliance relations, enabling measurement rather than just qualitative assessment. Trust level is derived from the ratio of monitoring investment to expected gains in the human-AI interaction. High trust: high expected gains with low monitoring costs. Low trust: monitoring costs approach or exceed expected gains. The framework also accommodates trust as either 'monitoring' (investing in verification to compensate for uncertainty) or 'confidence in reliability' (assuming the system is trustworthy without active verification). Applied to AI assurance, this means every high-stakes AI system should have a continuously updated dashboard showing: expected gains from AI use, monitoring investment required to maintain appropriate reliance, and the ratio between them. The goal is appropriate reliance — calibrated trust — not maximal trust.

tools.name: EU AI Agent Deployment Taxonomy (Nine Categories)
tools.description: A practical taxonomy of nine AI agent deployment categories developed by Nannini et al. (p171) that maps concrete agent actions to regulatory triggers under the EU AI Act. Each category identifies a different combination of autonomy level, tool-invocation scope, environmental impact, and human oversight requirement. The taxonomy is significant because it provides the first systematic mapping from agentic AI architectural patterns to specific regulatory obligations — allowing providers to identify which EU AI Act articles are triggered by specific agent configurations. The nine categories range from low-autonomy advisory agents to fully autonomous critical infrastructure agents, each with distinct compliance requirements. The taxonomy operationalises the principle that agent governance cannot be one-size-fits-all: different autonomy levels require different oversight architectures.

tools.name: CABCA Continuous Compliance Standard
tools.description: ETSI's CABCA (Continuous Compliance for Dynamic AI Systems) standard enables real-time compliance monitoring for AI systems that adapt post-deployment, directly addressing the point-in-time certification inadequacy for behavioral trust properties. The standard maps EU AI Act requirements (Article 9 risk management, Article 10 data governance, Article 12 technical documentation, Article 14 human oversight) to technically-auditable rules that can be checked continuously rather than at periodic audits. CABCA represents the regulatory engineering response to the temporal degradation problem: by creating standardised continuous monitoring infrastructure, it makes behavioral trust properties auditable over time rather than frozen at certification moments. Implementation requires AI systems to expose runtime telemetry sufficient to evaluate compliance state continuously.

tools.name: Runtime Behavioural Drift Detection
tools.description: A compliance architecture methodology from Nannini et al. (p171) that addresses the boundary between anticipated adaptive AI behaviour and 'substantial modification' under EU AI Act Article 3(23). Runtime behavioural drift detection requires: (1) baseline behavioural characterisation at deployment — defining the expected range of the agent's actions in each context; (2) continuous monitoring of actual versus expected behaviour — detecting when the agent's action patterns diverge from baseline; (3) change-point detection — identifying moments when the agent's strategy shifts discontinuously (the boundary between normal adaptation and substantial modification); (4) documentation and reporting — creating the evidentiary record needed for compliance and liability purposes. This methodology directly addresses the agentic AI governance gap: without runtime drift detection, it is impossible to know whether an agent has crossed from legitimate adaptation into a substantial modification that triggers new regulatory obligations.

tools.name: Reversal Register
tools.description: The primary governance artifact from the Dynamic Authority Reversal (DAR) framework (Westover et al., p195). A Reversal Register is an auditable log that binds each AI-assisted decision to: (1) the prevailing authority state at the time of decision (HL, AL, CO, or MO); (2) the trigger conditions that caused any state transition; (3) the actors (human or AI) in each role; (4) the thresholds used to evaluate trigger conditions; (5) the explanation delivered to relevant parties; (6) the action taken. The Reversal Register transforms accountability from a static property (who signed off) into a dynamic property (which authority state produced each decision, and why). It is the operational artifact that makes EU AI Act Article 14's 'meaningful oversight' requirement specific and auditable. In financial services, the Reversal Register provides the evidentiary basis for demonstrating proportionate human supervisory involvement under the three-pillar liability framework (p177) and for satisfying post-market monitoring obligations under EU AI Act Article 12.

tools.name: Short Trust in Automation Scale (S-TIAS)
tools.description: A 3-item psychometrically validated instrument for measuring human trust in AI systems (McGrath et al., 2025, p196). The S-TIAS was developed via factor analysis from the validated 12-item TIAS, retaining three items that capture the two dimensions of trust and distrust as oblique (related but distinct) factors. Validation studies confirm: (1) S-TIAS is valid and reliable across diverse AI applications (self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, virtual assistants); (2) trust measured by S-TIAS significantly predicts behavioural intention to use AI; (3) machine trust propensity (MTP) predicts S-TIAS trust under high-performance conditions. The S-TIAS enables frequent, minimally disruptive trust measurement in organisational settings — critical for longitudinal calibration monitoring under CABCA methodology. Recommended measurement points: deployment, 30 days, 90 days, and annually. Discrepancies between S-TIAS scores and objective system performance data indicate calibration problems requiring governance intervention.

tools.name: Dynamic Authority Reversal (DAR) Framework
tools.description: A governance framework that treats human-AI collaboration as a temporal authority problem with four states: Human-Leader/AI-Follower (HL), AI-Leader/Human-Follower (AL), Co-Leadership (CO), and Mutual Override (MO). Transitions between states are governed by four trigger classes: data superiority, contextual judgment requirements, risk thresholds, and ethics overrides. Key structural features: hysteresis bands (exit thresholds differ from entry thresholds to prevent oscillation between states), safe-exit timers (maximum dwell time in AL state triggers mandatory human review — directly addresses EU AI Act Article 14 meaningful oversight requirement), and the Reversal Register (audit log binding each decision to authority state, triggers, and justifications). The DAR framework is the operational complement to Baum et al.'s causal taxonomy of HITL (p187): where Baum et al. distinguish constitutive from corrective human involvement, DAR specifies the architectural mechanisms for implementing each type at scale.

tools.name: CABCA Operationalisation Toolkit
tools.description: Practical implementation guidance for ETSI TS 104 008 Continuous Auditing-Based Conformity Assessment (CABCA). The toolkit operationalises CABCA across five implementation layers: (1) Trigger Definition — defining the events that initiate a reassessment cycle (model drift detection, data distribution shift, hyperparameter change, capability level change, new deployment context); (2) Evidence Collection — automated collection of behavioural data sufficient to demonstrate conformity with applicable requirements, including model performance metrics, fairness indicators, drift statistics, and user trust calibration data; (3) Metric Operationalisation — translating high-level regulatory requirements into measurable, machine-readable specifications (e.g., EU AI Act Article 10 data governance requirements → specific data quality metrics with defined thresholds); (4) Automated Reporting — generating machine-readable conformity reports that map evidence to specific regulatory requirements, enabling regulatory submission without manual report preparation; (5) Continuous Review Cycle — establishing a perpetual review cycle with defined frequency for each property type (behavioural properties: continuous or weekly; structural properties: quarterly or upon triggering event). Note: TS 104 008 is a Technical Specification, not a Harmonised Standard — it provides methodology benefits but no legal presumption of conformity under EU AI Act (Smith, p199).

tools.name: AI Trust Calibration Audit Protocol
tools.description: An audit methodology for assessing whether organisational reliance on AI systems is appropriately calibrated to actual system performance. Based on Lugato and Floridi's appropriate reliance framework (p166) and McGrath's S-TIAS measurement (p196), the protocol has four steps: (1) Measure — administer S-TIAS to representative users of each AI system, collecting trust scores at multiple time points; (2) Compare — obtain objective system performance data (accuracy, calibration, fairness metrics, drift statistics) for the same period; (3) Calculate Calibration Error — compute the gap between measured trust and expected trust given system performance, identifying over-trust or under-trust; (4) Intervene — if calibration error exceeds defined thresholds, trigger governance intervention (user re-training, system modification, deployment restriction, or enhanced monitoring). The protocol is designed for integration with CABCA continuous monitoring and for use as the primary audit instrument for appropriate reliance assessment under the appropriate reliance framework.

tools.name: Trajectory Logging Infrastructure Specification
tools.description: A technical specification for the trajectory logging infrastructure required by trajectory-based liability frameworks (Bommarito et al., p192). The specification requires: (1) comprehensive action logging — every AI action (tool invocation, decision output, environmental interaction) logged with timestamp, inputs, outputs, and the decision state that produced it; (2) trajectory reconstruction capability — sufficient log granularity to reconstruct the full behavioural trajectory of the AI system for any time period, enabling regulators and courts to assess whether trajectories stayed within permissible bounds; (3) trajectory classification — automated classification of trajectories as within-bounds or out-of-bounds against defined behavioural specifications, with flagged trajectories escalated for human review; (4) audit access — secure, immutable log storage with regulated access protocols enabling authorised third-party review (regulators, courts) without exposing commercially sensitive model details. The March 2026 AI Liability Directive burden-of-proof shift makes trajectory logging infrastructure a legal necessity for deployers of autonomous AI systems, not merely a governance best practice.

tools.name: Reversal Register
tools.description: The primary governance artifact from the Dynamic Authority Reversal (DAR) framework (Westover et al., p195). A Reversal Register is an auditable log that binds each AI-assisted decision to: (1) the prevailing authority state at the time of decision (HL, AL, CO, or MO); (2) the trigger conditions that caused any state transition; (3) the actors (human or AI) in each role; (4) the thresholds used to evaluate trigger conditions; (5) the explanation delivered to relevant parties; (6) the action taken. The Reversal Register transforms accountability from a static property (who signed off) into a dynamic property (which authority state produced each decision, and why). It is the operational artifact that makes EU AI Act Article 14's 'meaningful oversight' requirement specific and auditable. In financial services, the Reversal Register provides the evidentiary basis for demonstrating proportionate human supervisory involvement under the three-pillar liability framework and for satisfying post-market monitoring obligations.

tools.name: Short Trust in Automation Scale (S-TIAS)
tools.description: A 3-item psychometrically validated instrument for measuring human trust in AI systems (McGrath et al., 2025, p202/p196). The S-TIAS captures the two dimensions of trust and distrust as oblique (related but distinct) factors. Validation studies confirm: (1) S-TIAS is valid and reliable across diverse AI applications; (2) trust measured by S-TIAS significantly predicts behavioural intention to use AI; (3) machine trust propensity (MTP) predicts S-TIAS trust under high-performance conditions. The S-TIAS enables frequent, minimally disruptive trust measurement in organisational settings — critical for longitudinal calibration monitoring. Recommended measurement points: deployment, 30 days, 90 days, and annually. Discrepancies between S-TIAS scores and objective system performance data indicate calibration problems requiring governance intervention.

tools.name: Dynamic Authority Reversal (DAR) Framework
tools.description: A governance framework treating human-AI collaboration as a temporal authority problem with four states: Human-Leader/AI-Follower (HL), AI-Leader/Human-Follower (AL), Co-Leadership (CO), and Mutual Override (MO). Transitions between states are governed by four trigger classes: data superiority, contextual judgment requirements, risk thresholds, and ethics overrides. Key structural features: hysteresis bands (exit thresholds differ from entry thresholds to prevent oscillation), safe-exit timers (maximum dwell time in AL state triggers mandatory human review — directly addresses EU AI Act Article 14 meaningful oversight requirement), and the Reversal Register (audit log binding each decision to authority state, triggers, and justifications). The DAR framework operationalises meaningful human oversight without requiring human review of every AI action.

tools.name: Resolution-Matched Supervision Architecture
tools.description: An architectural approach to human oversight of agentic AI systems where the scope and intensity of oversight is calibrated to the consequence profile of AI actions, not the volume. The key operational insight is that agentic AI generates too many actions for human review, but the distribution of consequences is highly skewed — most actions are low-consequence, a small fraction are medium-consequence, and a tiny fraction are high-consequence. Resolution-matched supervision means: (1) ensuring high-consequence actions are always subject to human review or pre-approval; (2) having automated alerting for medium-consequence exceptions; (3) accepting that low-consequence actions will be taken by AI without human review, with appropriate monitoring. The key concept is 'resolution bandwidth' — human oversight infrastructure has limited capacity, and that capacity must be allocated to the highest-resolution decisions.

tools.name: AI Trust Calibration Audit Protocol
tools.description: An audit methodology for assessing whether organisational reliance on AI systems is appropriately calibrated to actual system performance. Based on Lugato and Floridi's appropriate reliance framework (p166) and McGrath's S-TIAS measurement (p202/p196), the protocol has four steps: (1) Measure — administer S-TIAS to representative users of each AI system; (2) Compare — obtain objective system performance data (accuracy, calibration, fairness metrics, drift statistics) for the same period; (3) Calculate Calibration Error — compute the gap between measured trust and expected trust given system performance, identifying over-trust or under-trust; (4) Intervene — if calibration error exceeds defined thresholds, trigger governance intervention. The protocol enables continuous trust calibration monitoring as the primary assurance instrument.

tools.name: Trajectory Logging Infrastructure Specification
tools.description: A technical specification for the trajectory logging infrastructure required by trajectory-based liability frameworks (Bommarito et al., p192). The specification requires: (1) comprehensive action logging — every AI action (tool invocation, decision output, environmental interaction) logged with timestamp, inputs, outputs, and the decision state that produced it; (2) trajectory reconstruction capability — sufficient log granularity to reconstruct the full behavioural trajectory for any time period; (3) trajectory classification — automated classification of trajectories as within-bounds or out-of-bounds against defined behavioural specifications; (4) audit access — secure, immutable log storage with regulated access protocols. The March 2026 AI Liability Directive burden-of-proof shift makes trajectory logging infrastructure a legal necessity for deployers of autonomous AI systems.

tools.name: Constitutive Knowledge Source Mapping
tools.description: A methodology for grounding AI outputs in institutional constitutive knowledge sources — regulatory standards, diagnostic criteria, and legal frameworks that carry recognised epistemic authority within their domain (Gazit et al., p201). The methodology has four steps: (1) Constitutive Source Identification — for each high-stakes decision domain, identify the institutional sources that constitute what counts as valid within that domain (e.g., APRA/EBA regulatory standards for creditworthiness, clinical diagnostic criteria for medical AI); (2) Source Mapping — document which AI outputs are grounded in which institutional standards; (3) Consistency Verification — evidence that the AI system is producing outputs consistent with those sources; (4) Divergence Monitoring — a protocol for detecting when AI outputs diverge from constitutive source requirements. This is not a transparency requirement; it is an institutional grounding requirement that is auditable and proportionate.
